questions.text: I tend to miss when someone withdraws from a conversation as a silent protest.
I find that waiting for important news doesn't produce the anxiety I see in others.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the consideration that should inform the design of institutions as much as it informs individual conscience.
I maintain a steady emotional baseline regardless of what's happening around me.
I am energized by the process of building a decision together.
I believe the world would be better if more people put others ahead of themselves.
I find that rigid plans prevent me from noticing unexpected opportunities.
I hold the view that I owe it to myself to pursue the life I want.
I find that having a system for everything frees up mental energy for what matters.
I hold the view that pursuing what I want is not at odds with being a good person.
I tend to assert boundaries for the group about what is and isn't acceptable.
I prefer to collect others' ideas and find the common thread.
I redesign my approach each time I encounter a new problem rather than applying a formula.
I find that maintaining my emotional equilibrium during painful situations is what allows me to be present and useful over extended periods.
I am attuned to the split-second micro-expressions that reveal what someone actually thinks.
I prefer to deal with what is said rather than speculate about what was meant.
I find that I do my best thinking about human problems when I can set aside the emotional weight and focus on the facts.
My emotional states are so vivid that they feel almost cinematic.
